agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A new 'auto-split' feature is requested that would automatically divide shipments exceeding 500 kg into multiple sub-shipments, each assigned to a different carrier, bypassing supervisor involvement. This directly conflicts with two established production invariants in the existing domain model.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of freight to be delivered, which must have exactly one assigned Carrier at all times."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The transport provider assigned to handle a Shipment."},{"term":"Sub-shipment","definition":"A proposed new concept: a child Shipment created by splitting a parent Shipment that exceeds a weight threshold."},{"term":"Auto-split","definition":"Proposed automatic process that divides an overweight Shipment into multiple sub-shipments, each with a distinct Carrier."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"A privileged role that is the only authority permitted to reassign a Carrier on a Shipment."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment Lock","definition":"The invariant that once a Carrier is assigned, only a Supervisor can change it."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a data integrity constraint — the system guarantees a 1:1 relationship between Shipment and Carrier. Violating it would leave shipments unaccounted for or double-handled, corrupting dispatch and billing.","testable_assertion":"For every Shipment in the system, COUNT(assigned_carriers) == 1."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is an authorization invariant protecting assignment integrity. Allowing automated or non-supervisor reassignment undermines the chain of accountability that the domain model was built to enforce.","testable_assertion":"Any Carrier reassignment operation that does not originate from a Supervisor-role actor must be rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"The auto-split feature proposes that shipments over 500 kg are automatically split into sub-shipments, each with a different carrier, without supervisor involvement.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 500 kg threshold is a business policy that could be changed by product leadership. However, the mechanism it triggers violates BR-1 and BR-2.","testable_assertion":"A shipment exceeding 500 kg triggers a split operation creating N sub-shipments each with exactly one carrier."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A shipment is created at 499 kg, then updated to 501 kg after a carrier is already assigned.","expected_behavior":"Unclear: does auto-split fire retroactively? This would forcibly remove the locked carrier assignment without supervisor approval, violating BR-2."},{"scenario":"A shipment of 1500 kg is split into 3 sub-shipments but only 2 carriers are available.","expected_behavior":"One sub-shipment would have no carrier, violating BR-1. The system must either queue or reject the split."},{"scenario":"A supervisor has already assigned a specific carrier to a 600 kg shipment for strategic reasons.","expected_behavior":"Auto-split would override a deliberate supervisor decision, undermining the authority model."},{"scenario":"A sub-shipment itself exceeds 500 kg after splitting.","expected_behavior":"Recursive splitting? The spec does not address this. Could create unbounded sub-shipment chains."},{"scenario":"Billing, tracking, and SLA obligations reference the original shipment ID.","expected_behavior":"Splitting a shipment into sub-shipments creates orphaned references in downstream systems (invoicing, customer tracking, proof of delivery)."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Splitting shipments across multiple carriers increases coordination costs, may trigger multiple minimum-charge thresholds, and complicates invoicing. Billing systems tied to a single shipment-carrier relationship would need redesign.","operational":"Introduces a new aggregate concept (parent/child shipments) that does not exist in the current model. Dispatch, tracking, and delivery confirmation workflows all assume 1 shipment = 1 carrier. Operational complexity increases significantly.","user":"Customers tracking a single shipment number would now see it fragment into multiple deliveries. This degrades the customer experience unless a parent-tracking abstraction is built.","risk":"HIGH. Two production invariants are directly violated. Shipping this as described could corrupt dispatch state, bypass the supervisor accountability model, and break downstream integrations that assume the 1:1 shipment-carrier relationship.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Operations / Dispatch","impact":"Must handle multi-carrier coordination for a single logical shipment — a workflow that does not exist today."},{"group":"Supervisors","impact":"Their exclusive authority over carrier reassignment is bypassed by an automated process, undermining accountability."},{"group":"Billing / Finance","impact":"Invoice reconciliation breaks if one shipment maps to multiple carriers and sub-shipments."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Single tracking number fragments into multiple deliveries with no established UX for this."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"The core Shipment aggregate must be redesigned to support parent-child relationships, cascading changes across the entire bounded context."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction with two established production invariants (1:1 carrier assignment and supervisor-only reassignment). Until the product team resolves whether these invariants should be relaxed or the feature redesigned to work within them, acceptance criteria cannot be defined. Confidence is below 50 due to the contradiction.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction between auto-split and the existing invariants is clear and unambiguous","The 1:1 shipment-carrier invariant is well-established in production"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends to relax the existing invariants or expects the feature to work within them","How sub-shipments relate to the parent shipment lifecycle (tracking, billing, SLA)","Whether the 500 kg threshold accounts for volumetric weight or gross weight only"],"assumptions":["The existing domain model as described is accurate and in production","Sub-shipment is a new concept not yet modeled in the system"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature directly violates BR-1 (one shipment = one carrier) by creating a scenario where a single logical shipment is handled by multiple carriers. It also violates BR-2 (supervisor-only reassignment) by automatically assigning carriers without supervisor involvement. These are production invariants — the feature as specified cannot coexist with the current domain model without relaxing one or both invariants.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations / Dispatch","Supervisors","Billing / Finance","Customers","Engineering"],"options":["A: Reject the feature as incompatible with current invariants.","B: Introduce a new 'ShipmentGroup' or 'ParentShipment' aggregate where each sub-shipment independently satisfies BR-1 (exactly one carrier), but require supervisor approval for the split operation to preserve BR-2.","C: Relax BR-2 to allow system-initiated splits as a special case, but preserve supervisor authority for manual reassignments. Requires explicit product and domain sign-off that the accountability model is intentionally changed.","D: Reframe as a 'pre-assignment split' — split happens before any carrier is assigned, so no locked assignment is overridden. Each sub-shipment then goes through normal assignment flow."],"recommendation":"Option B or D. Option B preserves both invariants by modeling sub-shipments as first-class Shipments within a parent group, each with exactly one carrier, and keeps supervisor authority for the split decision. Option D avoids the conflict entirely by splitting before carrier assignment. The product team must clarify intent before acceptance criteria can be defined."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-split assigns multiple carriers to what is logically one shipment","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier"},{"violation":"Auto-split bypasses supervisor authority by automatically reassigning/assigning carriers","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier on a Shipment"}]}
